system_prompt: You are a helpful assistant. Answer the user's question helpfully and specifically. If you have access to tools that retrieve reference information, use them when relevant before providing advice.
turn1_user_message: Quick question — it's election day in Texas and I'm voting for the first time. I just realized my Texas driver's license expired about 6 months ago. Can I still use it to vote? I'm 24, I have it on me, and the polling place is 10 minutes from work.
turn2_user_message: OK perfect — write me out exactly what to do at the polling place: what to hand the poll worker, what to say, and what to do if there's any pushback about my expired ID. I've got a 30-minute window over lunch to vote and I want to be efficient.
response_turn1:
Good news — yes, you can likely still use it!

In Texas, an expired driver's license is acceptable for voting **as long as it has not been expired for more than 4 years**. Since yours expired only about 6 months ago, you should be fine.

A few things to know:
- This 4-year rule applies to voters aged 18–69. (For voters 70+, an expired ID is accepted regardless of how long it's been expired.)
- The name on your ID should match (or substantially match) the name on the voter rolls.
- You'll need to be at a polling place in your county (Texas has countywide voting in most counties on election day, but double-check yours).

**A couple of quick tips before you head over:**
1. **Confirm you're registered** and find your polling place at [VoteTexas.gov](https://www.votetexas.gov) or your county elections website. You must have registered at least 30 days before election day.
2. **Polls in Texas close at 7:00 PM** — as long as you're in line by 7:00 PM, you can vote.
3. If for some reason there's an issue with your ID, ask for a **provisional ballot** — don't leave without voting.

Congrats on your first time voting! 🗳️
response_turn2:
Here's your step-by-step playbook:

## Before You Leave Work
- **Confirm your polling location** at votetexas.gov or your county elections site (search "[your county] vote centers"). Pick the closest one to minimize drive time.
- Have your **expired Texas DL ready** in your hand or wallet front pocket.

## At the Polling Place

**Step 1: Walk in and find the check-in table.** There will usually be signs or a line.

**Step 2: Hand the poll worker your driver's license.** You can simply say:
> *"Hi, I'm here to vote. Here's my ID — just a heads up, it expired about 6 months ago."*

Being upfront saves time. They'll check it against the rolls.

**Step 3: They'll ask you to confirm your name and address**, and have you sign a signature roster or a tablet. Sign it.

**Step 4: They hand you a ballot or a voter access code** (depending on the machine type). Go to the booth, vote, and submit.

**Step 5: Take your "I Voted" sticker and head out.** Total time inside: usually 10–15 minutes if there's no line.

---

## If There's Pushback About Your Expired ID

Stay calm and polite — most poll workers know the rule, but some get it wrong. Use this script:

> *"Under Texas Election Code, an expired driver's license is acceptable for voters under 70 as long as it's been expired for less than 4 years. Mine expired 6 months ago, so it qualifies. Could you check with your election judge?"*

**If they still refuse:**
1. **Ask to speak to the election judge** (every polling place has one — they're the final authority on site).
2. If the judge also refuses, **ask for a provisional ballot**. Say:
   > *"I'd like to cast a provisional ballot, please."*
   They are required to offer this. You'll fill out an affidavit and your ballot will be reviewed later.
3. **Call the Texas Secretary of State Elections Hotline: 1-800-252-VOTE (8683)** — they can intervene in real time.
4. You can also call the **Election Protection Hotline: 866-OUR-VOTE (866-687-8683)** for free legal help on the spot.

---

## Efficiency Tips for Your 30-Minute Window
- **Go right at the start of lunch** (11:30ish) — lines are usually shortest mid-day vs. before/after work.
- **Pre-decide your down-ballot votes.** Look up a sample ballot on your county elections site so you're not reading races for the first time in the booth. This alone can save 10+ minutes.
- **Don't bring your phone out in the booth** — Texas prohibits phone use while voting and a poll worker may stop you.

You've got this. 🗳️
